title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Marine
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Federal alert sharpens: Iran-affiliated hackers are manipulating the PLCs that also run port and terminal OT
items.content_text: Federal agencies sharpened their warning that Iran-affiliated hackers are actively manipulating the programmable logic controllers behind US critical infrastructure — the same controllers that run cranes, fuel, power and water systems inside MTSA-regulated ports and terminals. Advisory AA26-097A, updated 22 July, confirms actors reached internet-exposed Rockwell, Schneider and Siemens gear, stole project files, and rewrote or deleted control logic, in some cases disabling shutdown and alarm functions without alerting operators. Port facilities are exactly the kind of mixed IT/OT environment the campaign favours: diverse controllers, remote maintenance access, and edge devices often reachable from the internet. The activity has already caused operational disruption and financial loss across critical-infrastructure sectors. It is opportunistic rather than sophisticated — the way in is weak credentials and controllers left exposed, not a novel exploit.

items.title: King Ocean Services discloses data breach at Caribbean/Latin America ocean carrier
items.content_text: Miami-based ocean carrier and logistics firm King Ocean Services disclosed on 11 July a data-security incident that exposed personal information, including government ID numbers, of affected individuals. King Ocean provides ocean transportation, trucking and warehousing across the Caribbean, Central and South America. The company has not detailed the cause, timeline or whether a third party was involved. The confirmed impact is IT/data-side rather than vessel or terminal OT, but it underlines that maritime logistics operators remain squarely in attackers' sights.

_otthreatfeed.source_name: King Ocean Services breach notice / ISSSource

items.title: Leaked Fortinet firewall credentials expose maritime, port and energy networks
items.content_text: Threat-intelligence reporting in early July flagged a “FortiBleed” exposure — large sets of leaked Fortinet firewall credentials — affecting maritime, port and energy organisations. For ports and shipping companies, Fortinet gear typically sits on the perimeter and the remote-access path used by shore-side IT and, increasingly, vessel connectivity. Valid credentials to that edge are a direct route toward the OT and logistics systems behind it, and they sidestep the need for an exploit entirely.

items.title: Maritime cyber incidents surged in 2025 as OT attacks and ransomware climbed
items.content_text: Sector threat reporting (Resecurity and others) documents a sharp rise in maritime cyber activity: 828 recorded incidents in 2025, roughly double the prior year, with ransomware cases more than doubling and attacks specifically on maritime operational technology up around 150%. The trajectory reflects a sector with expanding connectivity — ship-to-shore links, port automation, third-party managers — and uneven security maturity. The trend, not any single event, is the story: OT is increasingly in scope for maritime attackers.

items.title: GPS/GNSS spoofing remains a daily hazard for vessels and port operations
items.content_text: Coast Guard leadership and international partners continue to flag GPS spoofing and jamming as a persistent maritime safety threat, with disruptions reported on the order of a thousand a day affecting tens of thousands of vessels, and multiple European states warning of indiscriminate spoofing tied to electronic-warfare activity. This is interference rather than a network intrusion, but it degrades navigation, timing and AIS integrity that bridge and port systems depend on. Crews and terminals need manual fallback for position and timing.

items.title: FortiSandbox RCE flaws added to CISA KEV — the exploited edge on ports' remote-access path
items.content_text: CISA added two critical Fortinet flaws, CVE-2026-39808 and CVE-2026-25089 (both CVSS 9.1), to the KEV catalog on 16 July after evidence of in-the-wild exploitation. They are OS command-injection bugs in FortiSandbox / Cloud / PaaS enabling unauthenticated command execution via crafted HTTP requests. Combined with the leaked FortiGate credentials hitting maritime networks, this puts the Fortinet edge — often the boundary in front of port and vessel systems — under active pressure. CISA set a 19 July federal remediation deadline.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Added to KEV 16 Jul 2026 — actively exploited

items.title: USCG Marine Transportation System cyber rule: reporting and Cyber Security Officer duties in force
items.content_text: The US Coast Guard's Marine Transportation System cybersecurity rule continues to phase in for US ports and MTSA-regulated facilities. Reportable cyber incidents must be reported to the National Response Center (within 12 hours for incidents affecting seaworthiness or security compliance), and facilities must designate a Cyber Security Officer alongside the traditional Facility Security Officer. For operators the near-term work is governance: named accountability, incident-reporting playbooks, and a cyber plan that maps to the rule.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Rule in force since 16 Jul 2025 — phased compliance

items.title: IACS UR E26/E27 keep raising the cyber bar for newbuild vessels
items.content_text: The IACS Unified Requirements UR E26 (whole-ship cyber resilience) and UR E27 (equipment-level security) apply to vessels with construction contracts signed on or after 1 July 2024, covering identification, protection, detection, response and recovery across integrated IT/OT and third-party equipment. The requirements shift cyber resilience into ship design and supplier obligations, so owners, yards and integrators building 500 GT-plus tonnage need cyber baked into specifications, not bolted on later.
